quality_narrative.moat_rationale: QDRO has no discernible competitive advantage; it lacks profitability, financial stability, growth momentum, and any demonstrated ability to earn returns above its cost of capital.

metric_assessments.profitability: The company generates no measurable profit, reflected in a profit margin that cannot be calculated from available data. Without earnings, there is no foundation for shareholder returns or reinvestment in the business. This is the most fundamental problem facing the company.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: A current ratio of 0.04 means the company has only 4 cents in liquid assets for every dollar of short-term obligations—a critical liquidity crisis. The absence of debt-to-EBITDA data signals the company either has no EBITDA to measure or carries debt levels too severe to report meaningfully. This company cannot cover its near-term bills.
metric_assessments.growth: Zero projected earnings growth over the next five years, combined with absent revenue growth data, indicates the business is either stalled or in contraction. Without expansion in the top line or profitability improvement, there is no path to value creation. The company appears to be treading water at best.
metric_assessments.predictability: All key metrics—ROE, ROIC, revenue growth, and margins—are either zero or unavailable, making it impossible to forecast future performance with any confidence. A business that cannot be modeled is a business investors cannot evaluate. This opacity is itself a red flag.
metric_assessments.valuation: Without profit, growth, or asset backing to justify a price, any valuation is pure speculation. The company's financial distress likely means the stock price reflects distressed or near-worthless fundamentals. Cost basis is irrelevant when the underlying business is not generating returns.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: QDRO is rated Dangerous because it exhibits every hallmark of financial distress: a liquidity crisis (current ratio of 0.04), zero profitability, no growth trajectory, and metrics so weak they cannot be meaningfully measured. This is not a turnaround story with identifiable catalysts—it is a company in active financial trouble.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: QDRO Acquisition Corp. is a shell-stage financial services company with no operating earnings, no revenue growth, and a severe cash shortage that threatens its ability to meet obligations. This stock should be avoided by investors seeking stable, profitable businesses; it belongs in the portfolio of only experienced distressed investors with high risk tolerance and clear visibility into a recovery path—neither of which is evident here.

company_profile.name: QuidelOrtho Corporation
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: QuidelOrtho Corporation provides diagnostic testing solutions. The company operates through Labs, Transfusion Medicine, Point of Care, and Molecular Diagnostics business units. The Labs business unit provides clinical chemistry laboratory instruments and tests that measure target chemicals in bodily fluids for the evaluation of health and the clinical management of patients; immunoassay laboratory instruments and tests, to measure proteins as they act as antigens in the spread of disease, antibodies in the immune response spurred by disease, or markers of proper organ function and health; testing to detect and monitor disease progression across a spectrum of therapeutic areas; and specialized diagnostic solutions. The Transfusion Medicine business unit offers immunohematology instruments and tests used for blood typing to ensure patient-donor compatibility in blood transfusions; and donor screening instruments and tests used for blood and plasma screening for infectious diseases. The Point of Care business unit provides instruments and tests to provide rapid results across a continuum of POC settings. The Molecular Diagnostics business unit offers polymerase chain reaction thermocyclers; amplification systems; and sample-to-result molecular instruments and tests for syndromic infectious disease diagnostics. The company sells its products directly to end users through a direct sales force; and through a network of distributors for professional use in physician offices, hospitals, clinical laboratories, reference laboratories, urgent care clinics, universities, retail clinics, pharmacies, wellness screening centers, blood banks, and donor centers, as well as for individual, non-professional, and over-the-counter use. It operates in North America, Europe, the Middle East, Africa, China, Japan and Asia Pacific, and Latin America. The company was incorporated in 1979 and is headquartered in San Diego, California.
company_profile.description_summary:
Provides diagnostic testing solutions across labs, transfusion medicine, point of care, and molecular diagnostics. Offers clinical chemistry and immunoassay instruments, blood typing and donor screening systems, rapid point-of-care tests, and molecular diagnostic tools. Sells directly and through distributors to hospitals, clinics, laboratories, blood banks, and consumers globally.

Distinctive for comprehensive diagnostic portfolio spanning traditional laboratory testing to advanced molecular diagnostics, enabling disease detection and monitoring across multiple clinical settings and therapeutic areas.

final_score_pct = max(0, final_pct − penalty)

company_profile.name: Quince Therapeutics, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Quince Therapeutics, Inc., a biotechnology company, focuses on acquiring, developing, and commercializing therapeutics for patients with debilitating and rare diseases. The company's lead asset candidature comprises eDSP, which is in Phase 3, for the treatment of rare pediatric neurodegenerative disease, including A-T, an inherited autosomal recessive neurodegenerative and immunodeficiency disorder caused by mutations in ATM gene. Its AIDE technology platform, a drug/device combination platform that uses an automated process to encapsulate a drug into a patient's own red blood cells, as well as consist of an automated equipment the RCL, a sterile single-use consumable treatment kit comprising kit medical devices, syringe kit, and process solutions. The company was formerly known as Cortexyme, Inc. and changed its name to Quince Therapeutics, Inc. in August 2022. The company was incorporated in 2012 and is headquartered in South San Francisco, California.
company_profile.description_summary:
Quince Therapeutics is a biotechnology company developing therapeutics for rare and debilitating diseases. Its lead candidate, eDSP, is in Phase 3 trials for ataxia-telangiectasia, a rare pediatric neurodegenerative disorder. The company generates potential revenue through drug development and commercialization efforts.

The company's distinctive AIDE technology platform encapsulates drugs into patients' own red blood cells using an automated process, combining pharmaceutical and device components for enhanced therapeutic delivery.

company_profile.type_rationale: Consumer finance / payments — DNI and P/B prioritised; standard debt ratios unsuitable due to balance sheet structure
company_profile.description: Qfin Holdings, Inc., together with its subsidiaries, operate AI- driven credit-tech platform under the Qifu Jietiao brand in the People's Republic of China. The company provides credit-driven services that match borrowers with financial institutions to conduct borrower acquisition, credit assessment, fund matching, and post-facilitation services; and platform services, including loan and post-facilitation services to financial institution partners under capital light model, intelligence credit engine, referral services, and other technology solutions. It serves financial institutions, consumers, and small and micro-enterprises. The company was formerly known as Qifu Technology, Inc. and changed its name to Qfin Holdings, Inc. in July 2025. Qfin Holdings, Inc. was founded in 2016 and is headquartered in Shanghai, the People's Republic of China.
company_profile.description_summary:
Qfin operates an AI-driven credit-tech platform in China that connects borrowers with financial institutions. It generates revenue through credit-driven services including borrower acquisition, credit assessment, and fund matching, plus platform services offering loans and technology solutions to financial institution partners.

The company distinguishes itself through its capital-light model powered by an intelligent credit engine, serving financial institutions, consumers, and small businesses across China's lending market.

company_profile.description: QUALCOMM Incorporated engages in the development and commercialization of foundational technologies for the wireless industry worldwide. It operates through three segments: Qualcomm CDMA Technologies (QCT); Qualcomm Technology Licensing (QTL); and Qualcomm Strategic Initiatives (QSI). The QCT segment develops and supplies integrated circuits and system software with connectivity and computing technologies for use in mobile devices; automotive systems for connectivity, digital cockpit, and ADAS/AD; and IoT, including consumer electronic devices, industrial devices, and edge networking products. The QTL segment grants licenses or provides rights to use portions of its intellectual property portfolio, which include various patent rights useful in the manufacture and sale of wireless products comprising products implementing LTE, and/or OFDMA-based 5G products and derivatives; to use cellular standard-essential patents, including 3G, 4G and 5G for cellular devices. The QSI segment invests in early-stage companies in various industries, including 5G, artificial intelligence, automotive, consumer, enterprise, cloud, IoT, and extended reality, and investments, including non-marketable equity securities and, to a lesser extent, marketable equity securities, and convertible debt instruments. It also provides development, and other services and sells related products to the United States government agencies and their contractors. In addition, the company is also involved in Qualcomm government technologies and data center businesses. QUALCOMM Incorporated was incorporated in 1985 and is headquartered in San Diego, California.
company_profile.description_summary:
Qualcomm develops and commercializes wireless technologies globally across three segments. Its QCT segment designs semiconductors and software for mobile devices, automotive systems, and IoT products. QTL licenses its extensive patent portfolio covering 3G, 4G, and 5G cellular standards. QSI invests in early-stage companies across emerging technologies.

The company generates revenue from both product sales and intellectual property licensing, a distinctive dual-revenue model. It also serves U.S. government agencies and operates in government technologies and data center businesses.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (technology) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: Qualcomm commands a strong competitive moat anchored by exceptional profitability (36% ROE, 22% margins) and solid returns on invested capital, though predictability weakness and revenue headwinds suggest the moat faces cyclical pressure rather than being unshakeable.

metric_assessments.profitability: A 36% return on equity is exceptional—most companies aim for 15-20%, so Qualcomm is operating in the top tier of industrial efficiency. The company is converting revenue into profits at a 22% rate, which sits well above the 10-15% typical for technology firms, showing strong pricing power and cost discipline in its chip design and licensing business. However, this excellence is being tested by shrinking revenue (-3.5% year-over-year), which means the company is squeezing more from less, a tactic that cannot persist indefinitely without fresh growth.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: With a current ratio of 2.82, Qualcomm has $2.82 in liquid assets for every $1 of short-term obligations—a healthy cushion that puts it in above-average footing for the sector. The debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 1.17 is moderate and manageable, typical for a cyclical tech company, and does not constrain the balance sheet. The main limiting factor is that this 7/10 score reflects adequate but not fortress-like strength; a truly strong company would carry less leverage or show faster cash conversion, leaving some room for improvement in a downturn.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue is declining 3.5% annually, and earnings per share are expected to shrink by 0.5% over the next five years—both red flags for a company in a growth-hungry sector. This 5/10 score reflects stagnation rather than momentum; the business is profitable but not expanding, which is typical for a mature chipmaker caught between smartphone saturation and cyclical chip-market weakness. The company's growth is explicitly constrained (per the data flag), meaning management and market headwinds are both limiting expansion, and no dramatic turnaround is priced into forward expectations.
metric_assessments.predictability: Qualcomm's earnings are harder to forecast because it operates in a cyclical industry where smartphone demand and chip inventory swings create lumpy results, and new competitive pressures in mobile chips add uncertainty. A 4/10 predictability score reflects this reality—the business is solid, but the timing of profits depends heavily on customer demand cycles and competitive wins that are difficult to predict years ahead. The company's strong margins and profitability are real, but they fluctuate with industry conditions rather than grinding steadily upward, making it unsuitable for investors who need highly consistent earnings visibility.
metric_assessments.valuation: At a 3/10 valuation score, the stock is trading at a premium relative to its current earnings and growth prospects, meaning you are paying a lot today for a company growing slowly or not at all. For a stock with negative near-term growth (-3.5% revenue, -0.5% EPS), trading at a high price multiple is a poor bargain; investors are essentially betting on a turnaround that has not yet materialized. This weak valuation score is the biggest headwind for new buyers, as the risk-reward today tilts toward disappointment unless the company can reignite growth or cut its valuation in line with its slowing momentum.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Qualcomm earns the 'Safe' classification because its profitability and balance sheet are genuinely strong—it generates excellent returns and carries manageable debt. However, safety here means 'not a financial wreck,' not 'risk-free'; the company is cyclical, growth is stalled, and the stock is pricey, so your returns will depend more on timing and a business turnaround than on owning a predictable cash machine. It is a quality business, but at a risky price.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Qualcomm is a world-class chip designer with exceptional profitability—earning 36% returns on shareholder equity and 22% profit margins put it among the best operators in technology—and a solid balance sheet that can weather downturns without distress. The company's dominance in smartphone modem and 5G chip design remains a real competitive advantage, but it is being overshadowed by revenue declines of 3.5% and stalled earnings growth over the next five years, reflecting sluggish smartphone demand and intense competitive pressure in its core markets. Predictability is weak (4/10) because Qualcomm's profits swing with industry chip cycles, making it hard to forecast earnings confidence; cyclical businesses at this volatility level are riskier than they appear in calm years. The stock is expensive relative to its growth (valuation 3/10), meaning you would be paying a premium price for a company in a holding pattern—a poor entry point unless you are confident in a near-term turnaround. For most beginners, Qualcomm is a 'wait and see' story: own it if you believe 5G and AI chips will reignite growth soon, but avoid it if you prefer to buy quality at reasonable prices.

company_profile.description: Qualys, Inc. provides cloud-based platform delivering information technology (IT), security, and compliance solutions in the United States and internationally. The company offers its solutions to enterprises, government entities, and small and medium-sized businesses in various industries, including education, financial services, government, healthcare, insurance, manufacturing, media, retail, technology, and utilities through its sales teams, as well as through security consulting organizations, managed service providers, resellers, cloud providers, and consulting firms. company_profile.description_summary:

_meta.type_rationale: FCF compounder — FCF margin 34.7%; DCF methods prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Free cash flow compounders are businesses that consistently generate more cash than they need to run the business — think of companies like consumer goods brands or software firms with subscription revenue. Because their cash flows are reliable year after year, a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model works very well. DCF asks: if I add up all the future cash flows this company will generate and adjust for the time value of money, what is it worth today? For these companies, that calculation gives a meaningful and trustworthy answer.

company_profile.description_summary:

Generates revenue through eFPGA IP licensing, discrete FPGA devices, silicon products, development tools, software, and professional services. The company distinguishes itself through end-to-end AI/machine learning solutions featuring accurate sensor algorithms, combined with comprehensive eFPGA integration services that enable customers to embed programmable logic into custom semiconductor designs.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (technology) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (technology) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

quality.note: Moat scored by Haiku API — final_score_pct computed out of 50.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: Qorvo has moderate competitive moat strength, supported by solid profitability and returns on capital around 10%, but constrained by weak predictability, modest margins of 9.2%, and exposure to cyclical semiconductor demand that limits pricing power.

metric_assessments.profitability: A 9.2% profit margin is solid but not exceptional—it sits in the middle range for semiconductor and RF component companies, where leaders often post 12–18%. The company is generating reasonable profits from each dollar of revenue, which reflects efficient operations, but the margin is being held back by competitive pricing pressure in chipsets and the need for heavy R&D spending to stay competitive in RF technology.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: With a current ratio of 3.24 and debt-to-EBITDA of 1.97, Qorvo has a healthy liquidity cushion and manageable debt levels typical of large-cap tech companies. A 1.97x debt-to-EBITDA ratio is well within safe territory for a cyclical business, meaning the company can service its obligations comfortably even during industry downturns—this is what earns the strong 8/10 score.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue declined 7% year-over-year, signaling that Qorvo is in a contraction phase tied to the broader semiconductor cycle, not a structural growth problem. The modest 8% expected EPS growth over the next five years reflects recovery from this trough but not breakout expansion; the company is constrained by its exposure to cyclical end-markets like smartphones and 5G infrastructure, where demand can swing sharply based on carrier and consumer spending cycles.
metric_assessments.predictability: A 5/10 score reflects the inherent unpredictability of a cyclical chip company—earnings swing with industry cycles, and it's hard to forecast when demand will bounce back or peak. Returns on capital of 10.06% are respectable but modest, suggesting the business doesn't have the durable competitive advantages that would let investors forecast earnings with high confidence; margins and volumes fluctuate more than a compounder's would.
metric_assessments.valuation: At a 6/10, the stock appears fairly valued relative to its growth prospects and returns, neither a bargain nor a warning sign. The 8% five-year EPS growth outlook paired with a profit margin of 9.2% and 10% ROIC suggests the market is pricing in recovery without excessive optimism, though valuation becomes more attractive only if revenue growth returns to positive territory.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Qorvo is rated Speculative because it combines solid financial footing (strong balance sheet, decent profitability) with material cyclical and execution risks that make the outcome less certain than blue-chip stocks. The company is currently in a revenue downturn, predictability is only adequate, and its competitive advantages in RF components are not fortress-like enough to guarantee stable returns through industry cycles, making it better suited for investors who can tolerate short-term swings.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Qorvo is a well-capitalized semiconductor and RF component maker with strong balance-sheet health and respectable profitability, but it is working through a cyclical downturn that has cut revenue 7% year-over-year and revealed modest earning power relative to the semiconductor industry's best. The main risk is that recovery depends on smartphone and 5G infrastructure spending bouncing back on a timeline no one can predict with certainty, and the company's 10% return on invested capital leaves little room for execution errors. Growth is expected to average 8% annually over the next five years as demand stabilizes, but this is a recovery play rather than a growth story—the valuation at current levels is fair, not cheap, and offers no margin of safety to offset the cyclical risk. For most beginning investors, this is a hold-and-watch situation: the business quality is respectable but not exceptional, and the stock is best suited for patient investors who believe in the 5G cycle's durability and can stomach interim volatility.
quality_narrative.quality_conclusion: Qorvo, Inc. demonstrates mixed fundamental quality and benefits from solid financial strength.

_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

company_profile.name: Q2 Holdings, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: FCF compounder — FCF margin 21.9%; DCF methods prioritised
company_profile.description: Q2 Holdings, Inc. provides digital solutions to financial institutions, financial technology companies, FinTechs, and alternative finance companies (Alt-FIs) in the United States. The company offers Digital Banking Platform, an end-to-end digital banking platform that supports its financial institution customers in their delivery of retail, SMB, and commercial functionalities across digital channels; and risk and fraud solutions that are designed to support financial institutions' efforts to protect end users, comply with regulatory requirements and manage fraud risk efficiently. It also provides Q2 Innovation Studio, an application program interface and software development kit based open technology platform that allows financial institution customers, FinTechs, and other partners to deploy customized experiences and financial services to end users; and Helix, a cloud-native, real-time core processing platform that combines the services and functionality for companies and financial institutions. In addition, the company offers digital lending and relationship pricing solutions that support financial institutions, FinTechs and Alt-FIs in managing lending workflows, pricing strategies and customer relationships across commercial and consumer use cases, as well as offers loans, deposits and fee-based products. The company was formerly known as CBG Holdings, Inc. and changed its name to Q2 Holdings, Inc. in March 2013. Q2 Holdings, Inc. was founded in 2004 and is headquartered in Austin, Texas.
company_profile.description_summary:
Q2 Holdings provides digital banking solutions to financial institutions and fintech companies. Its primary revenue comes from a Digital Banking Platform that enables retail, SMB, and commercial banking across digital channels, complemented by risk and fraud solutions, core processing technology, digital lending tools, and relationship pricing services.

The company differentiates itself through specialized platforms like the Q2 Innovation Studio for customized financial experiences and Helix, a cloud-native core processing system designed for modern financial operations.

_meta.type_rationale: FCF compounder — FCF margin 21.9%; DCF methods prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Free cash flow compounders are businesses that consistently generate more cash than they need to run the business — think of companies like consumer goods brands or software firms with subscription revenue. Because their cash flows are reliable year after year, a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model works very well. DCF asks: if I add up all the future cash flows this company will generate and adjust for the time value of money, what is it worth today? For these companies, that calculation gives a meaningful and trustworthy answer.

company_profile.name: QCR Holdings, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Bank or thrift — P/Tangible Book prioritised; DCF excluded
company_profile.description: QCR Holdings, Inc., a multi-bank holding company, provides commercial and consumer banking, and trust and asset management services. The company's deposit products include noninterest-bearing demand, interest-bearing demand, time, and brokered deposits. It also provides various commercial and retail lending/leasing, and investment services to corporations, partnerships, individuals, and government agencies. In addition, the company offers loans to small and mid-sized businesses; business loans, including lines of credit for working capital and operational purposes; term loans for the acquisition of facilities, equipment, and other purposes; commercial and residential real estate loans; and installment and other consumer loans, such as motor vehicle, home improvement, home equity, signature loans, and small personal credit lines, as well as issuance of trust preferred securities. QCR Holdings, Inc. was incorporated in 1993 and is headquartered in Moline, Illinois.
company_profile.description_summary:
A multi-bank holding company providing commercial and consumer banking services alongside trust and asset management. Revenue derives from deposit products, commercial and retail lending, leasing, and investment services to corporations, individuals, and government agencies.

The company focuses on small and mid-sized business lending, offering lines of credit, term loans, and real estate financing, while also providing consumer loans including mortgages, auto loans, and personal credit lines.

_meta.type_rationale: Bank or thrift — P/Tangible Book prioritised; DCF excluded
_meta.method_explanation: Banks make money from the difference between what they charge on loans and what they pay on deposits — not from traditional business cash flows. Because of this, standard cash flow models (DCF) do not work well for banks. Instead, we focus on Price-to-Book (P/B), which compares the stock price to the value of the bank's assets on its books. Banks also carry a lot of debt by design — they borrow money to lend it out — so high leverage is normal and not a red flag.

company_profile.name: Quantum Corporation
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: Quantum Corporation provides products for storing and managing digital video and unstructured data in the Americas, Europe, the Middle East, Africa, and the Asia Pacific. The company offers Myriad All-Flash File and Object Storage for enterprise unstructured data applications such as AI, machine learning, and data analytics; Unified Surveillance Platform Software that unified compute and storage for video surveillance recording, storage, and analytics; StorNext Hybrid Flash/Disk File Storage Software for video editing, post-production, and streaming applications, as well as digital file archives; and CatDV Asset Management Software for indexing, cataloging, AI data, and workflow. It also provides ActiveScale Object Storage Software, a scalable and durable storage; DXi Backup Appliances to build backup appliances for backup and recovery and multisite data protection; and Scalar Tape Storage that secure storage for long term data archiving and offline data protection which are used by hyperscalers and enterprises. In addition, the company sells standalone linear-tape open tape drives for small business data protection and archiving; and linear-tape open media for use in tape storage systems. Further, it offers global support, customer support agreements, software subscriptions, installation, education, and consulting and training services; and Quantum-as-a-Service. Additionally, the company offers cloud-based analytics, standard plans, professional, managed, and storage-as-a-service. The company sells its products through a network of distributors, value-added resellers, direct marketing resellers, original equipment manufacturers, and other suppliers, as well as directly to corporate entities and government agencies. The company has a strategic partnership with Entanglement, Inc. to advance quantum logic, post-quantum security, and data sovereignty. Quantum Corporation was incorporated in 1980 and is headquartered in Centennial, Colorado.
company_profile.description_summary:
Quantum provides digital storage and data management solutions for enterprises globally, specializing in unstructured data, video surveillance, and archival systems. The company distinguishes itself through comprehensive offerings spanning multiple storage technologies—from flash and disk to tape—and vertical-specific solutions for video production, AI analytics, and long-term archiving, supported by managed services and cloud-based options.

metric_assessments.predictability: The combination of massive losses, zero earnings growth expectations, and poor liquidity makes future results impossible to forecast with confidence.
